_meta.description: Interactive Stage Explorer content tree. Informational only, not medical advice; prices are 2026 reference figures, confirm with providers.

home.title: Where are you in your journey?
home.intro: Tap a stage to explore. Each step opens up more detail, prices and guidance — and you can always go back.

options.label: Pre-conception & Testing
options.hint: Trying, or think you might be pregnant?

preconception.title: Pre-conception & Testing
preconception.intro: Just starting out, or think you might be pregnant? Begin here.
body: Whether you're planning ahead or waiting to test, a few simple steps set you up well for a healthy start.

options.label: Pregnancy test kits
options.hint: Where to buy, prices & how to compare

options.label: Start folic acid
options.hint: What, when and where to get it

options.label: Choosing a doctor / hospital
options.hint: GP vs O&G, public vs private

test-kits.title: Home pregnancy test kits
test-kits.intro: How they work, when to test, and what they cost in Singapore.
body: A home pregnancy test checks your urine for the hormone hCG, which rises after a fertilised egg implants.
Most kits are most reliable from the first day of your missed period; some 'early' kits detect a few days sooner.

priceTable.caption: Common test kits in Singapore (indicative)
priceTable.note: Indicative ranges — confirm current prices at Watsons, Guardian, NTUC FairPrice, Shopee.sg or Lazada.sg.

test-kits.tip: For best accuracy, test with your first-morning urine, when hCG is most concentrated.

options.label: How to use a test kit

how-to-test.title: How to use a test kit
how-to-test.intro: A few simple steps for a reliable result.
body: 1. Read the leaflet that comes with your kit — brands differ slightly.
2. Use your first-morning urine for best accuracy.
3. Either hold the stick in your urine stream or dip it, as the kit instructs.
4. Lay it flat and wait the stated time (often 3–5 minutes).
5. Read the result within the time window — lines that appear much later can be misleading.
how-to-test.tip: Two lines (or 'Pregnant') = positive; one line (or 'Not Pregnant') = negative — even a faint second line usually counts as positive.

test-results.title: Understanding your result
test-results.intro: What does your test show? Tap the one that matches.

result-positive.celebrate: 🎉 Congratulations! A positive result is the start of an incredible journey.
result-positive.title: You're pregnant — what now?
result-positive.intro: Here are your first, gentle next steps.
body: A positive home test is usually accurate. The next move is to confirm it and begin early care so you and your baby get the best start.
result-positive.tip: Book your first appointment with a GP or O&G — they'll confirm the pregnancy and guide you from here.

options.label: What to expect at the dating scan

result-negative.title: A negative result
result-negative.intro: Not the end of the road — timing matters.
body: A negative can simply mean it's too early and hCG isn't high enough to detect yet.
If your period doesn't arrive, retest in 2–3 days with first-morning urine.
If your cycles are irregular, or you've been trying for a while, it's worth speaking to a doctor.

options.label: Back to test kits
options.hint: Retest or try a more sensitive kit

result-unclear.title: Faint line or unclear result
result-unclear.intro: Very common — here's what it usually means.
body: A faint second line is often an early positive, when hCG is still low.
Retest in 48 hours with first-morning urine — the line usually gets clearer if you're pregnant.
For certainty, a clinic can do a blood test, which detects pregnancy earlier and more precisely.

options.label: See a clinic for a blood test

first-visit.title: Your first prenatal visit
first-visit.intro: What happens, and what to bring.
body: Usually around 6–8 weeks. The doctor confirms the pregnancy, reviews your health history, may do a urine test and an early scan, and estimates your due date.
Bring your NRIC/ID and a list of any medications or supplements you take.

dating-scan.title: The dating scan
dating-scan.intro: Your first look at your baby.
body: Usually 6–10 weeks. It confirms the pregnancy is in the womb and developing, checks the heartbeat, and gives an accurate due date based on the baby's size.

folic-acid.title: Folic acid
folic-acid.intro: A small daily step with a big effect.
body: The WHO and Singapore's Health Promotion Board recommend folic acid from pre-conception through about week 12 to support healthy neural development. Prenatal multivitamins usually include it.

priceTable.caption: Where to get it (indicative)
priceTable.note: Indicative — ask your doctor or pharmacist what's right for you.

choose-gynae.title: Choosing a doctor or hospital
choose-gynae.intro: Public or private, GP or O&G — here's the gist.
body: You can start with a GP, or go straight to an O&G (obstetrician-gynaecologist).
Public hospitals (KKH, NUH, SGH) generally cost less and can handle higher-risk care; private care offers continuity with one doctor and more flexible timings.
The full comparison — packages and estimated costs — lives on the Hospitals page of the site.

first-tri.title: First Trimester (Weeks 1–12)
first-tri.intro: Here's what to look out for in these early weeks.
body: Big things are happening even though there's little to see yet. These are the key milestones and checks.

screening.title: First-trimester screening
screening.intro: OSCAR and NIPT — what they are.
body: OSCAR (~11–14 weeks) combines an ultrasound with a blood test to assess the chance of certain conditions.
NIPT is a newer, more accurate blood test that can be done from about 10 weeks. Your doctor will help you decide which suits you.

priceTable.caption: Reference screening costs (2026)
priceTable.note: 2026 reference (MOH benchmarks / provider rates) — some costs MediSave-claimable. Confirm with your provider.

symptoms.title: Common early symptoms
symptoms.intro: Mostly normal — but know the red flags.
body: Nausea, tiredness, tender breasts and needing to pee often are all common and usually settle.
Contact your doctor promptly if you have heavy bleeding, severe or constant pain, or vomiting so bad you can't keep fluids down.
symptoms.tip: Trust your instincts — if something feels wrong, it's always OK to call your provider.

anomaly-scan.title: The anomaly scan
anomaly-scan.intro: A detailed check of your baby's development.
body: Around 18–22 weeks, this scan checks the baby's organs and growth. If you'd like, you can usually find out the sex.

glucose-test.title: Glucose tolerance test (GTT)
body: Around 24–28 weeks, you drink a sugary solution and have blood taken to check how your body handles glucose.

kicks.title: Feeling kicks
body: Many first feel movement around 18–24 weeks. Later on, getting to know your baby's pattern helps you notice changes.

third-tri.title: Third Trimester (Weeks 28–40)
third-tri.intro: The home stretch — time to prepare.

hospital-bag.title: Hospital bag checklist
body: For you: ID & documents, comfy clothes, toiletries, phone charger, snacks. For baby: going-home outfit, mittens, swaddle. Your hospital provides most essentials — check its list too.

birth-plan.title: Your birth plan
birth-plan.intro: A guide to your preferences — kept flexible.
body: Jot down preferences for pain relief, who's with you, and after-birth care, then talk them through with your provider. Plans can change on the day, and that's OK.

gbs.title: GBS swab
body: Around 35–37 weeks, a simple swab checks for Group B Strep, a common bacteria that can be managed easily during labour if present.

labour.title: Labour & Delivery
labour.intro: Knowing the signs helps you feel ready.

signs.title: Signs of labour
signs.intro: Call your hospital or doctor when these start.
body: Regular, tightening contractions that get closer together; your waters breaking; or a 'show' (a small mucus plug). If contractions are strong and regular, or your waters break, contact your hospital.
signs.tip: If you have heavy bleeding or reduced baby movements, call your provider immediately.

admission.title: Getting admitted
admission.intro: What to expect at the hospital.
body: Staff check your contractions and the baby, may examine how far labour has progressed, and settle you into the delivery ward. Bring your hospital bag and documents.

after-birth.title: Right after birth
body: Skin-to-skin contact, the first feed, and initial checks for your baby. Lactation support is available at most Singapore hospitals if you'd like help.
